questions.question: Why is the trait `OrderedCollection<T: Ord>` instead of just `OrderedCollection<T>`?
options: Trait bounds enforce that T supports comparison at the type level. The compiler rejects `OrderedCollection<MyStruct>` if MyStruct doesn't implement `Ord`. The impl can then use < and > freely.
questions.explanation: Rust generics need bounds to know what operations are available on T. `T: Ord` says 'T must implement Ord' — gives access to <, >, ==, cmp. Without it, the compiler doesn't know if T can be compared. Bound at the trait level means every impl AND every consumer of OrderedCollection sees this requirement uniformly.

questions.question: What's the role of `debug_assert!` in the capstone library?
options: Encodes invariants that are checked in DEBUG builds (cargo build) but compiled away in RELEASE builds (cargo build --release). Free in production; loud during testing.
questions.explanation: After every push/pop, the MinHeap's heap invariant is verified — O(n) per operation. In debug this catches bugs immediately (corrupted heap → instant panic). In release it's compiled away, so production performance is the same as without the check. This is the canonical 'invariant-driven development' pattern from Phase 2 L17.

questions.question: Why does the capstone use `Option<Box<Node<T>>>` for linked-list nodes?
options: Owned, nullable, heap-allocated. `Option` encodes the maybe-null in the type system. `Box` is the heap pointer. `Node<T>` is the payload. Together: safe Rust's idiomatic linked-list cell.
questions.explanation: Rust doesn't allow raw nullable pointers in safe code. `Option<T>` carries the 'has value or not' bit at the type level — no null-deref bugs possible. `Box<T>` is owned heap memory. Together, `Option<Box<T>>` is precisely 'a pointer that might be null'. Drop semantics free the whole chain when the head is dropped — no manual memory management.

questions.question: After 10K random inserts, why is the AVL set's height ~17-18?
options: AVL keeps height ≤ 1.44 × log₂(n+2). For n=10000, this is ~19.4. The observed height stays under this bound — that's the AVL guarantee.
questions.explanation: AVL's strict height-balanced invariant (bf in {-1, 0, +1}) bounds height at 1.44 log₂(n+2). For n=10K: ~19.4. Random inserts produce trees near this bound (~17-18). Compare with a plain BST: height could be 10K (degenerate chain) on adversarial input. AVL's `debug_assert!(verify_rec(...).0)` confirms the invariant after every insert.

questions.question: What does Phase 3 build culminate in (across all 25 lessons)?
options: A complete data-structure toolkit: linear (Vec, list, queue), tabular (hash map), tree (BST, AVL, RB, B-tree), graph, probabilistic (Bloom, CM), concurrent (Treiber). The capstone integrates the core into one library; Phase 4 algorithms USE these structures.
questions.explanation: Phase 3's 25 lessons sweep the data-structure design space. Every algorithm in Phase 4 (sorting, searching, DP, graph algos) reaches for one of these primitives. Every system in Phase 7-11 (OS, compilers, networks, DBs, distributed) uses many. The capstone library is the minimum-viable distillation: 5 structures, generic, invariant-checked, ~400 lines of safe Rust.

questions.question: Why does the capstone include only Vec, list, heap, AVL, hash map — not all 24 structures?
options: These are the 'load-bearing' structures: every system you'll build needs at least one. Trees (B-tree, RB), specialized structures (suffix tree, segment tree, Bloom) are domain-specific; learning them is valuable but they're not core to every project.
questions.explanation: There's a power-law of which DS you actually use. ~80% of programs use Vec, hash map, and one ordered structure. ~15% need a heap (priority queue) somewhere. ~5% reach for B-trees, tries, suffix arrays, etc. The capstone reflects this: build the bread-and-butter rigorously, leave the specialized to per-domain implementations.

_meta.title: Phase Capstone A Generic Ds Library In Rust With Invariants
